title: Systems for vehicular platooning and methods therefor
abstract: Systems, methods, controllers and algorithm suitable for use in at least partially automated or autonomous vehicle control are described. The described schemes are well suited for use in vehicle platooning and/or vehicle convoying applications. In one aspect a vehicle mass estimator is described. In another aspect, a controller fault detector is described. In another aspect, a driver input device for use in engaging a platoon and dissolving a platoon is described.
claims:
Claims CLAIMS  What is claimed is: 1. A method of estimating a mass of a vehicle, the method comprising: obtaining vehicle sensor information from a vehicle while the vehicle is driving, the vehicle sensor information including at least one of wheel speed data and GPS position data; obtaining engine operating information indicative of at least engine torque and engine speed estimates for a period corresponding to the obtained vehicle sensor information; verifying that the obtained vehicle sensor information was not obtained while the vehicle was traveling at less than a predetermined threshold speed, wherein vehicle sensor information obtained while the vehicle was traveling at less than the predetermined threshold speed is not used in the mass estimation; verifying that the obtained vehicle sensor information was not obtained during a vehicle braking event, wherein vehicle sensor information obtained during a vehicle braking event is not used in the mass estimation; estimating the mass of the vehicle while the vehicle is driving based at least in part on the vehicle sensor information, and the engine operating information. 2. A method as recited in claim 1 further comprising: identifying a transmission gear that the vehicle is in when the vehicle sensor information is obtained; verifying that the obtained vehicle sensor information was not obtained during a transmission shift event, wherein vehicle sensor information obtained during a transmission shift event is not used in the mass estimation; and wherein the vehicle mass estimation is further based at least in part on the identified transmission gear. 3. A method as recited in claim 1 or 2 further comprising passing the vehicle sensor information through a finite impulse response (FIR) filter prior to using the vehicle sensor information in the vehicle mass estimation. 4. A method as recited in claim 3 wherein the FIR filter is a Hamming filter. 5. A method as recited in any of claims 1-4 further comprising obtaining vertical acceleration data for a period corresponding to the obtained vehicle sensor 

 information and eliminating any obtained vehicle sensor information that corresponds to detected vertical acceleration above a predetermined threshold. 6. A method as recited in any of claims 1-5 wherein vehicle sensor information corresponding to a designated period after a braking event is not used in the mass estimation. 7. A method of detecting a fault in a controller used in at least partially autonomous vehicle control, the method comprising: receiving an oscillating signal outputted from the controller; detecting edges of the oscillating signals; starting a timer when an edge is not detected as expected, triggering an alarm when the timer reaches after a designated value when no edge is detected prior to the triggering of the alarm, wherein the alarm is not activated in an edge is detected prior to triggering the alarm 8. A method as recited in claim 7 wherein the oscillating signal is a square wave heartbeat signal. 9. A method as recited in claim 7 or 8 wherein the detected edges are rising edges or falling edges or both rising and falling edges. 10. A controller fault detector for detecting a fault in a controller used in at least partially automated vehicle control, the controller fault detector comprising: an edge detector arranged to detect edges in an oscillating signal output by the controller; a timer that is triggered when the edge detector fails to detect an edge as expeted; an alarm that is triggered when the timer reaches a predetermined threshold after triggering without detecting an edge in the oscillating signal. 11. A controller fault detector as recited in claim 10 further comprising a super capacitor arranged to trigger the alarm in the event that the controller experiences a power outage. 12. A driver input device for use in engaging a platoon and dissolving a platoon, the driver input device including: 

 a engage/dissolve switch configured to be pressed by a driver to initiate a platoon when a platoon is not active, and to dissolve a platoon when the platoon is active; and a switch illumination light configured to illuminate in at least two different colors to provide a driver with a visual indication of a current platooning state, the different colors corresponding to different platooning states.
